stories.hook: Earth shows the clouds before the payoff
stories.lead: Earth glows at night because sunlight bounces off ice and clouds and back into space. That’s why astronauts see bright swirls over poles and deserts. Watch closely—those darker patches. Land absorbing light instead of reflecting. That’s the planet breathing. Ever notice how deserts shine the brightest.

stories.lead: Snakes taste the air with their forked tongues. They flick it in and out, collecting scent particles because that's why their tongues are forked—two points to triangulate smells. stories.source_description: Shows scenic points of interest in Yosemite National Park. A carload of tourists drives into the park under Arch Rock. Includes closeups of Bridal Veil Falls, El Capitan, Cathedral Spires, "Three Brothers," Upper and Lower Yosemite Falls, Mirror Lake, Mt. Watkins, Happy Isles, Merced River, Mariposa Grove, Wawena Tunnel Tree, Glacier Point Hotel, "Half-Dome," "The Lookout," Overhanging Rock, "Sentinel Dome," Vernal Falls, and "Split Rocks." stories.lead: Roofs are storm engines because every bolt of lightning is a 30,000-degree plasma wire. That's why steel rods in concrete don't just hold buildings—they channel strikes straight to ground. Watch this bolt hit a rod and the concrete just hisses. So next time thunder growls, thank the storm for welding your roof tighter.
